turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: In case 17-1307, Obduskey v. McCarthy & Holthus, Justice Breyer has the opinion of the Court.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: There is a federal Act called the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act which makes unlawful a whole host of unfair or abusive debt collection practices. Now, the question in this case is how much of the Act, i.e., does most of the Act, does it or does it not apply to a business that is engaged primarily in the non-judicial foreclosure of mortgages, sort of a technical case. Home mortgages are a kind of security interest, but in foreclosing on a home when a borrower does not make the required payments is a form of what is called in the statute Security Interest Enforcement, because they have a security interest in the home through the mortgage and these people enforce it. Now, every state has a system of judicial foreclosure, you go to Court, and about half the states have a system as well of non-judicial foreclosure, which is a fairly complicated system, but it does not involve going to Court very often. The petitioner here is a borrower, who borrowed some money, he is the mortgager, he gave a mortgage for it and he thinks that respondent, which is a law firm hired to pursue non-judicial foreclosures on behalf of the lenders, the people who did not pay when they were supposed to, failed to meet some of the Act's requirements. The Tenth Circuit held that the relevant portion of the Act does not cover this kind of activity or this firm, and we agree. Our basic reason for agreeing stems from the statute's language. That language when you first read it might seems that it covered these people, people who enforce security interests, because it defines a debt collector within the Act broadly as meaning 'any person in any business the principal purposes of which is the collection of any debts or who regularly collects or attempts to collect directly or indirectly debt', and one could consider a firm that specializes in foreclosing mortgages to be a firm that 'collects' or attempts to collect directly or indirectly debts. But, and the but is quite important here, the definitional part of the Act then goes on to say, for the purposes of §1692(f)(6) and that happens to be a separate section of the Act that deals specifically with non-foreclosure taking of property, it says for the purposes of §1692(f)(6) the term debt collector 'also includes any person in any business the principal purpose of which is the enforcement of security interest.' And there would not be any need for this extra sentence if those same people were already covered under the broader definition of the Act which would include that §1692(f)(6) and everything else. So in our opinion, we analyze the statutory language I just quoted in greater detail than I have here, we point out that Congress may have wished to exempt security interest enforces for most of the Act in order to avoid conflicts with state regulation, and for those of us who find legislative history useful, we find support for that few in the history. And we add that many states have laws that in the mortgage foreclosure context already offer borrower's protection. For these reasons and others which we elaborate in our opinion, we hold that the federal Act's requirements, except of course for those specified in §1692(f)(6), do not apply to businesses engaged in only non-judicial foreclosure proceedings. The opinion is unanimous. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurrence.
